elements.sent-2411-001: In this paper, we will construct formulas and bounds for Neighborhood Degree-based indices of graphs and describe graphs that attain the bounds.
elements.sent-2411-002: Furthermore, we will establish a lower bound for the spectral radius of any graph.
elements.sent-2411-003: In the last forty years, many scientists have developed mathematical models for analyzing structures and properties of various chemical compounds.
elements.sent-2411-004: Graph theory plays a very important role in developing many types of models such as Quantitative Structure-Property Relationships (QSPR) models, and Quantitative Structure-Activity Relationships (QSAR) models (\cite{BGG ,\cite{GFS , \cite{DB ,\cite{TC ).}}}}
elements.sent-2411-005: In molecular graphs of chemical compounds, vertices correspond to atoms and edges correspond to the bonds between them.
elements.sent-2411-006: A topological index (connectivity index) is a type of a molecular descriptor that is based on the molecular graph of a chemical compound.
elements.sent-2411-007: In 1947 Harry Weiner introduced a topological index related to molecular branching.
elements.sent-2411-008: He correlated the indices with the boiling points of certain chemical compounds, alkanes.
elements.sent-2411-009: This inspired many mathematicians and chemists to develop more topological indices for molecular graphs.
elements.sent-2411-010: In 1972, Zagreb indices were introduced by Gutman and Trijanastic \cite{GT .}
elements.sent-2411-011: They are based on the degrees of the vertices.
elements.sent-2411-012: They are very useful in modeling chemical and biological properties of chemical compounds, as shown by Klein, D.. J.
elements.sent-2411-013: Gutman et al \cite{GFMG and Zhou et al \cite{B used Zagreb indices for analyzing bounds for Estrada index, which is based on the eigenvalues of the adjacency matrix.}}
elements.sent-2411-014: Zagreb indices are generalized by many mathematicians and scientists.
elements.sent-2411-015: In 2019, Mondal et al \cite{MP introduced the general neighborhood Zagreb index and proved its importance in predicting properties of chemical compounds.}
elements.sent-2411-016: This index is based on the neighborhood degree of a vertex, which is the sum of the degrees of its adjacent vertices.
elements.sent-2411-017: In the last five years, many scientists used the neighborhood degree based indices to model various properties of chemical compounds which are used in medicines.
elements.sent-2411-018: For example, Mondal et al \cite{MP2 , investigated neighborhood degree based indices of some chemical compounds like remdesivir (GS-5734) which is useful for treatment of Covid-19 patients.}
elements.sent-2411-019: In 2023, Balasubramaniyan and Chidambaram \cite{BC , used these indices for analysis of asthma drugs.}
elements.sent-2411-020: The main research question is how to compute bounds and formulas of the indices stated above and find graphs which attain the bounds.
elements.sent-2411-021: In this paper, we will establish formulas for the general neighborhood Zagreb index.
elements.sent-2411-022: Additionally, we will find upper and lower bounds, and describe graphs which attain the bounds.
elements.sent-2411-023: Hu-Li-Shi-Xu-Gutman \cite{HLSXG investigated the zeroth-order general Randic index (which is the same as general Zagreb index) for molecular graphs in which the maximum vertex degree is at most 4.}
elements.sent-2411-024: In Theorem \ref{T:NB2 of this paper we will extend this further to the general neighborhood Zagreb index for graphs with maximum vertex degree more than 4.}
elements.sent-2411-025: In 2004, Yu-Lu-Tian \cite{YLT established a lower bound for the spectral radius of any graph, which is the largest eigenvalue of its adjacency matrix.}
elements.sent-2411-026: They used the 2-degree of a vertex, which is the sum of the degrees of the adjacent vertices.
elements.sent-2411-027: So the 2-degree is the same as the neighborhood degree of a vertex.
elements.sent-2411-028: This is amazing! Using this and our lower bound for general neighborhood Zagreb index, we will find a lower bound for spectral radius of any graph in Section 4.
elements.sent-2411-029: Additionally, using generalization of Benakli, Halleck, and Kingan \cite{BHK , we could define 2-distance neighborhood degree, $\delta_2(v)$, of any vertex v as the sum of the degrees of the vertices which are at distance 2 from the vertex v, which opens more research problems.}
elements.sent-2411-030: In Section 2 of this paper, we will review definitions of the first Zagreb index and general neighborhood Zagreb index.
elements.sent-2411-031: In Section 3 we will establish formulas of the general neighborhood Zagreb index for graphs.
elements.sent-2411-032: Additionally, we will find upper and lower bounds for the index and determine graphs which attain the bounds.
elements.sent-2411-033: In addition, we will establish sharp upper and lower bounds for the index for some special graphs.
elements.sent-2411-034: In Section 5 we will have discussion and conclusion.
elements.sent-2411-035: We will use the notation and terminology introduced in Gutman et al \cite{GFMG and Mondal et al \cite{MP .}}
elements.sent-2411-036: In {\bf molecular graphs of chemical compounds, vertices correspond to individual atoms and edges correspond to the bonds between them.}
elements.sent-2411-037: A {\bf topological index (connectivity index) is a type of a molecular descriptor that is based on the molecular graph of a chemical compound.The definition of the first Zagreb index, which is introduced by Gutman and Trijanastic \cite{GT , is as follows.}}
elements.sent-2411-038: Let $G$ be a graph with vertex set $V(G)$ and $d(u)$ be the degree of the vertex $u\in V(G)$.
elements.sent-2411-039: Then the first Zagreb index is defined as follows: \begin{equation}Z_G=M_1(G)=\sum_{u\in V(G)}d(u)^2.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-040: In 2007 Gutman-Fortula-Marković-Glišić \cite{GFMG proved that for a chemical tree (alkane) with $n$ vertices $Z_G=M_1(G)=6n-10-2n_2-2n_3$, where $n_i$ is the number of vertices of degree $i$ for i = 2, 3.}
elements.sent-2411-041: In Vaidya and Surendran \cite{VS , we established formulas of the first Zagreb index for molecular graphs of cycloalkanes, alkenes, and alkynes.}
elements.sent-2411-042: Zagreb indices have been generalized and studied for more than 30 years.
elements.sent-2411-043: In 2019, Mondal et al \cite{MP introduced the following general neighborhood Zagreb index.}
elements.sent-2411-044: Let $G$ be a graph with vertex set $V(G)$.
elements.sent-2411-045: For a vertex $u\in V(G)$, let $\delta_G(u)$ be its neighborhood degree, which is the sum of the degrees of the vertices which are adjacent to the vertex $u$.
elements.sent-2411-046: Let $\alpha$ be a nonzero real number such that $\alpha\neq 1$.
elements.sent-2411-047: Then the general neighborhood Zagreb index is defined as follows: \begin{equation}NM_\alpha (G)=\sum_{u\in V(G)}\delta_G(u)^\alpha.\end{equation}.
elements.section-2411-003: Formulas and bounds for Neighborhood Degree based indices
elements.sent-2411-048: In this section we will establish some formulas for the general neighborhood Zagreb index.
elements.sent-2411-049: We will then find sharp upper and lower bounds for it using these formulas.
elements.sent-2411-050: First, we need the following Lemma which is proved in Vaidya-Chang \cite{VC .}
elements.sent-2411-051: Let $p$ and $q$ be any positive integers such that $p < q$.
elements.sent-2411-054: If the real number $\alpha<0$ or $\alpha>1$, then $(p + i)^\alpha - p^\alpha - i(\frac{q^\alpha - p^\alpha {q - p ) \leq 0$ for $1 \leq i \leq q - p - 1$ and if $0 < \alpha < 1$, then $(p + i)^\alpha - p^\alpha - i(\frac{q^\alpha - p^\alpha {q - p ) \geq 0$ for $1 \leq i \leq q - p - 1$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-055: Let $G$ be a graph with $n$ vertices and $m$ edges where $n\geq 3$.
elements.sent-2411-056: Let $n_i$ denote the number of vertices of neighborhood degree $i$.
elements.sent-2411-057: Let $\delta$ and $\Delta$ respectively denote the minimum and the maximum neighborhood degree.
elements.sent-2411-060: The general neighborhood Zagreb index \begin{equation}NM_{\alpha}(G)=n\delta^\alpha + (M_1 - n\delta)s_\alpha +\sum_{i =1}^{\Delta - \delta - 1}n_{\delta + i} \left[(\delta + i)^\alpha - \delta^\alpha - is_\alpha\right].\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-061: Additionally, if $\alpha<0$ or $\alpha>1$ the coefficients $(\delta + i)^\alpha - \delta^\alpha - is_\alpha \leq 0$ for $1 \leq i \leq \Delta -\delta - 1$ and for $0 < \alpha < 1$, the coefficients $(\delta + i)^\alpha - \delta^\alpha - is_\alpha \geq 0$ for $1 \leq i \leq \Delta -\delta - 1$.
elements.sent-2411-062: The general neighborhood Zagreb index \begin{equation}NM_{\alpha}(G)=n\delta^\alpha + (M_1 - n\delta) ( (\delta + 1)^\alpha - \delta^\alpha) + \sum_{i = 2}^{\Delta - \delta }n_{\delta + i} \left[(\delta + i)^\alpha - \delta^\alpha - i((\delta + 1)^\alpha - \delta^\alpha)\right].\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-063: Additionally, if $\alpha<0$ or $\alpha>1$, the coefficients $(\delta + i)^\alpha - \delta^\alpha - i((\delta + 1)^\alpha - \delta^\alpha) \geq 0$ for $2 \leq i \leq \Delta -\delta$ and for $0 < \alpha < 1$, the coefficients $(\delta + i)^\alpha - \delta^\alpha - i((\delta + 1)^\alpha - \delta^\alpha) \leq 0$ for $2 \leq i \leq \Delta -\delta $.
elements.sent-2411-064: We have $\sum_{i= \delta ^\Delta in_i= M_1$ by part (i) of Lemma 1 of \cite{MDP .}}
elements.sent-2411-066: Solving these equations, we will rewrite $n_\Delta$ as follows.. \begin{equation}n_\Delta=\frac{M_1 - n\delta - \sum_{i=\delta + 1}^{\Delta - 1}((i - \delta)n_i}{\Delta - \delta}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-067: Write $NM_{\alpha (G)$ in terms of $n_i$, $\delta\leq i\leq \Delta$, and substitute $n_\delta$ with $n-\sum_{i=\delta + 1 ^\Delta n_i$, we obtain \begin{equation}NM_{\alpha} (G)=\sum_{i=\delta}^\Delta i^\alpha n_i = \delta^\alpha( n - \sum_{i= \delta + 1}^\Delta n_i) + \sum_{i=\delta + 1}^\Delta i^\alpha n_i = n\delta^\alpha + \sum_{i = \delta + 1}^\Delta n_i(i^\alpha - \delta^\alpha).\end{equation}.}}
elements.formula-2411-006: \begin{equation}NM_{\alpha} (G)=\sum_{i=\delta}^\Delta i^\alpha n_i = \delta^\alpha( n - \sum_{i= \delta + 1}^\Delta n_i) + \sum_{i=\delta + 1}^\Delta i^\alpha n_i = n\delta^\alpha + \sum_{i = \delta + 1}^\Delta n_i(i^\alpha - \delta^\alpha).\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-068: Further substitute $n_\Delta$ into the sum and using Lemma \ref{Lemma , we get the result in part (1).}
elements.sent-2411-069: Using the identities $n_\delta+n_{\delta + 1 = n - \sum_{i = \delta + 2 ^\Delta n_i$ and $\delta n_\delta + (\delta + 1)n_{\delta + 1 = M_1 - \sum_{i = \delta + 2 ^\Delta in_i$, we solve for $n_\delta$ and $n_{\delta + 1}$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-070: Substituting $n_\delta$ and $n_{\delta + 1}$ into $NM_{\alpha (G)=\sum_{i=\delta ^\Delta i^\alpha n_i$, and using Lemma \ref{Lemma again, we get the result in part (2).}}}
elements.sent-2411-071: The following corollary gives upper bounds or lower bounds for the general Zagreb index, depending on the values of $\alpha$.
elements.sent-2411-073: If $\alpha<0$ or $\alpha>1$, the upper bound for the general neighborhood Zagreb index is $ NM_{\alpha (G) \leq n\delta^\alpha + (M_1 - n\delta)s_\alpha$.}
elements.sent-2411-074: If $0<\alpha<1$, it becomes the lower bound for the general first Zagreb index, that is, $ NM_{\alpha (G) \geq n\delta^\alpha + (M_1 - n\delta)s_\alpha$.}
elements.sent-2411-075: Moreover, for parts (1) and (2), the equality occurs for any graph with vertices of neighborhood degrees $\delta$ and $\Delta.$.
elements.sent-2411-076: If $\alpha<0$ or $\alpha>1$, then the lower bound for the general neighborhood Zagreb index is $NM_{\alpha (G) \geq n\delta^\alpha + (M_1 - n\delta) ((\delta + 1)^\alpha - \delta^\alpha) + n_{\Delta (\Delta^\alpha - \delta^\alpha - (\Delta - \delta) ((\delta + 1)^\alpha - \delta^\alpha))$.}}
elements.sent-2411-077: If $0<\alpha<1$, then the upper bound for the general neighborhood Zagreb index is $NM_{\alpha (G) \leq n\delta^\alpha + (M_1 - n\delta) ((\delta + 1)^\alpha - \delta^\alpha) + n_{\Delta (\Delta^\alpha - \delta^\alpha - (\Delta - \delta) ((\delta + 1)^\alpha - \delta^\alpha))$.}}
elements.sent-2411-078: Moreover, for parts (3) and (4), the equality occurs if the graph G is a path.
elements.sent-2411-081: In Figure \ref{fig:1 , $\delta=4, \Delta = 10, m=n=12$ and all vertices have either neighborhood degree $4$ or $10$.}
elements.sent-2411-082: It attains the bounds given in parts (1) and (2) of Corollary \ref{C:NB .}

elements.sent-2411-083: In Theorem (2.2) of \cite{HLSXG , Hu-Li-Shi-Xu-Gutman established formulas for the maximum and the minimum zeroth-order general Randic index (which is same as general Zagreb index) for $(n, m)$ molecular graphs if $2m - n$ is congruent to $0, 1,$ or $2$ modulo $3$ and the maximum vertex degree is $4$.}
elements.sent-2411-084: In the following Theorem, we will extend this and find sharp bounds for the general neighborhood Zagreb index if $M_1 - n \delta$ is congruent to any integer $r$ modulo $\Delta - \delta$ where $0 \leq r \leq \Delta - \delta- 1$ and the maximum neighborhood degree $\Delta \geq 3 $.
elements.sent-2411-085: Assume that $\Delta - \delta\geq 2$ and $M_1 - n\delta = q(\Delta - \delta) + r$, where q is a positive integer and r is an integer such that $0 \leq r \leq \Delta - \delta - 1$.
elements.sent-2411-087: If $r = 0$ and $n_{\Delta = q$, then the graph G is a bi-neighborhood degree graph with vertices of neighborhood degree $\delta$ and $\Delta.$.}
elements.sent-2411-088: If $r \geq 1$ and $n_{\Delta = q$, then $n_i = 0$ for $\delta + r + 1 \leq i \leq \Delta - 1$ and $n_{\delta + r \leq 1$.}}
elements.sent-2411-089: If $r \geq 1$ and $\alpha<0$ or $\alpha>1$ and $n_{\delta + r \not = 0$ , then the upper bound for the general neighborhood Zagreb index is $NM_{\alpha (G)\leq n\delta^\alpha + (M_1 - n\delta)s_\alpha +\ (\delta + r)^\alpha - \delta^\alpha - rs_\alpha$.}}
elements.sent-2411-090: If $r \geq 1$ and $0<\alpha<1$ and $n_{\delta + r \not = 0$, it becomes the lower bound for the general neighborhood Zagreb index, that is, $NM_{\alpha (G)\geq n\delta^\alpha + (M_1 - n\delta)s_\alpha +\ (\delta + r)^\alpha - \delta^\alpha - rs_\alpha$.}}
elements.sent-2411-091: Additionally, in both parts (3) and (4) the equality occurs if the graph G has $q$ vertices of neighborhood degree $\Delta$, a unique vertex of neighborhood degree $\delta + r$, and $n - q - 1$ vertices of neighborhood degree $\delta$.
elements.sent-2411-092: By solving the equations, we get $\sum_{i=\delta + 1 ^\Delta (i - \delta)n_i= M_1 - n\delta = q(\Delta - \delta) + r$.}
elements.sent-2411-093: If $r = 0$ and $n_{\Delta = q$, then $n_i = 0$ for $ \delta + 1 \leq i \leq \Delta - 1$.}
elements.sent-2411-095: Also if $r \geq 1$ and $n_{\Delta = q$,then $n_i = 0$ for $\delta + r + 1 \leq i \leq \Delta - 1$ and $n_{\delta + r \leq 1$.}}
elements.sent-2411-098: By part (1) of Theorem \ref{T:NB we have the general neighborhood Zagreb index $NM_{\alpha (G)=n\delta^\alpha + (M_1 - n\delta)s_\alpha +\sum_{i =1 ^{\Delta - \delta - 1 n_{\delta + i \left[(\delta + i)^\alpha - \delta^\alpha - is_\alpha\right].$ If $\alpha<0$ or $\alpha>1$, then $(\delta + i)^\alpha - \delta^\alpha - is_\alpha \leq 0$ for $1 \leq i \leq \Delta -\delta - 1$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-099: So $NM_{\alpha (G) \leq n\delta^\alpha + (M_1 - n\delta)s_\alpha + n_{\delta + r [(\delta + r)^\alpha - \delta^\alpha - (r)s_\alpha].$ Since $n_{\delta + r \not = 0$, the result in part (3) follows.}}}
elements.sent-2411-100: Similarly, the result in part (4) follows since for $0<\alpha<1$, $(\delta + i)^\alpha - \delta^\alpha - is_\alpha \geq 0$ for $1 \leq i \leq \Delta -\delta - 1$.
elements.sent-2411-101: In addition, by part (1) of Theorem (3.2), for both parts (3) and (4) the equality occurs if the graph G has $q$ vertices of neighborhood degree $\Delta$, a unique vertex of neighborhood degree $\delta + r$, and $n - q - 1$ vertices of neighborhood degree $\delta$.
elements.sent-2411-102: For the graph in Figure \ref{fig:2 , we have $n = 5, \delta = 3, \Delta = 5$ and $M_1 - n\delta = 7 = 3(\Delta - \delta) + 1$.}

elements.section-2411-004: Spectral Radius, neighborhood degree, and its generalization
elements.sent-2411-105: In 2004, Yu-Lu-Tian \cite{YLT established a lower bound for the spectral radius, $\rho (G)$ of any graph $G$, which is the largest eigenvalue of its adjacency matrix.}
elements.sent-2411-107: Using this and part (2) of Theorem \ref{T:NB , we get the following Theorem.}
elements.sent-2411-108: Let $\delta$ denote the minimum neighborhood degree.
elements.sent-2411-109: Then the spectral radius, $\rho^2 \geq \frac {M_1(2\delta +1) - n\delta^2 - n\delta {M_1}$.}
elements.sent-2411-110: Follows from Theorem 4 of Yu-Lu-Tian \cite{YLT and part 2 of Theorem \ref{T:NB .}}
elements.sent-2411-111: Further we could define the general 2-distance neighborhood degree index as $NM2_\alpha (G)=\sum_{u\in V(G)}\delta_2(u)^\alpha$.
elements.sent-2411-112: In the following Theorem, we will establish some formulas for the 2-distance neighborhood degree index if the diameter of the graph is 2.
elements.sent-2411-113: Let $G$ be a graph of diameter 2 with $n$ vertices and $m$ edges where $n\geq 3$.
elements.sent-2411-114: Let $n_i$ denote the number of vertices of 2-distance neighborhood degree $i$.
elements.sent-2411-115: Let $d$ and $D$ respectively denote the minimum and the maximum 2-distance neighborhood degree.
elements.sent-2411-118: The 2-distance neighborhood degree index \begin{equation}NM2_\alpha(G)=nd^\alpha + (2m(n - 1) - M_1 - nd)S_\alpha +\sum_{i =1}^{D - d - 1}n_{d + i} \left[(d + i)^\alpha - d^\alpha - iS_\alpha\right].\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-007: \begin{equation}NM2_\alpha(G)=nd^\alpha + (2m(n - 1) - M_1 - nd)S_\alpha +\sum_{i =1}^{D - d - 1}n_{d + i} \left[(d + i)^\alpha - d^\alpha - iS_\alpha\right].\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-119: The 2-distance neighborhood degree index \begin{equation}NM2_\alpha(G)=nd^\alpha + (2m(n - 1) - M_1 - nd) ((d + 1)^\alpha - d^\alpha)+ \sum_{i = 2}^{D - d }n_{d + i} \left[(d + i)^\alpha - d^\alpha - i((d + 1)^\alpha - d^\alpha)\right].\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-008: \begin{equation}NM2_\alpha(G)=nd^\alpha + (2m(n - 1) - M_1 - nd) ((d + 1)^\alpha - d^\alpha)+ \sum_{i = 2}^{D - d }n_{d + i} \left[(d + i)^\alpha - d^\alpha - i((d + 1)^\alpha - d^\alpha)\right].\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-120: Solving these equations for $n_d$ and $n_D$ we get (1).
elements.sent-2411-121: Additionally, by solving the equations for $n_d$ and $n_{d + 1}$, we get (2).
elements.sent-2411-122: In conclusion, we established formulas of the general neighborhood Zagreb index for graphs.
elements.sent-2411-123: Additionally, we found upper and lower bounds for the general neighborhood Zagreb index and determine graphs which attain the bounds.
elements.sent-2411-124: In addition, we established sharp upper and lower bounds for the general neighborhood Zagreb index for some special graphs.
elements.sent-2411-125: We also found lower bound for the spectral radius.
elements.sent-2411-126: Additionally, we discussed generalization of the neighborhood degree which leads to more research problems.
elements.sent-2411-127: Since there are more than 100 topological indices, there are many open problems of finding bounds and formulas for them.
elements.sent-2411-128: They play a crucial role in modeling various properties of chemical compounds.
elements.sent-2411-129: This process of mathematical modeling is very useful in real life because if reduces cost and saves time in developing medicines.
elements.sent-2411-130: It is fascinating to see that neighborhood degree based indices are useful in finding bounds for spectral radius as well as predicting physico-chemical properties of chemical compounds.. \bibliographystyle{amsplain.}
